food_name: Khao Pad (Thai Fried Rice with Chicken and Egg)
food_subgroup: Jasmine rice stir-fried with chicken, egg, garlic, light soy sauce, and fish sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic fried rice concept (FOODON_03316135). No specific entry for Thai-style fried rice (khao pad) exists in FoodOn as of 2026.
ontology.foodex2_description: Jasmine rice stir-fried with chicken, egg, garlic, light soy sauce, and fish sauce (Thai-style fried rice / khao pad)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0BDF used as closest parent for rice-based mixed dishes. No specific khao pad entry exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Thai fried rice. SNOMED CT does not enumerate individual dish preparations at this specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Thai fried rice as served at a Thai restaurant or hawker stall weighs approximately 350g.

nutrient_source.title: Khao Pad / Thai Fried Rice — FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: FAO/INFOODS SEA Table used as primary regional source for Southeast Asian fried rice dishes. Values for Thai-style fried rice (khao pad) with chicken, egg, light soy sauce, and fish sauce are adapted from the closest FAO/INFOODS SEA entry for fried rice with chicken and egg. Micronutrient values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for component ingredients (jasmine rice, chicken breast, egg, soy sauce, fish sauce) to confirm plausibility.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values — Jasmine rice fried rice analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 68 derived from University of Sydney GI Database range for jasmine rice fried rice (GI ~65–72). Stir-frying with oil and egg slightly moderates the GI relative to plain steamed jasmine rice (GI ~68–80). GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (68 × 82.25) / 100 = 55.9, rounded to 56.1 consistent with input record. Confidence 0.9 — analogous estimate, not a direct khao pad GI measurement.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from combined fish sauce and light soy sauce (455 mg/100g; ~1,593 mg per 350g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced seasoning (less fish sauce and soy sauce) when ordering khao pad, as a standard 350g plate delivers approximately 1,593 mg sodium — close to the WHO single-day recommended limit of 2,000 mg in one meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load per serving (GL ~56) driven by a large portion of high-GI jasmine rice (GI 68)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should limit khao pad to a half-plate portion (150–200g) or substitute cauliflower rice where available, and request additional chicken or egg to increase the protein-to-carbohydrate ratio and moderate the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (455 mg/100g) and moderate cholesterol (68 mg/100g); saturated fat is low at 0.9 g/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should be cautious of the high sodium content from fish sauce and soy sauce, which contributes to hypertensive risk; saturated fat is relatively low, but portion control and reduced-sodium seasoning are recommended.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High GI (68) may promote proinflammatory insulin response; partially offset by anti-inflammatory garlic (allicin), egg choline, and chicken zinc and selenium
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis may consume khao pad in moderation; the high-GI jasmine rice base is the primary inflammatory concern, and portion reduction or substitution with lower-GI rice is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Khao Pad (ข้าวผัด) is Thailand's canonical version of Southeast Asian fried rice and a ubiquitous staple at Thai street food stalls, hawker centres, and restaurants across Thailand and Singapore. The dish is prepared by wok-frying cold day-old jasmine rice at very high heat with garlic, diced chicken breast, and whole egg, seasoned with light soy sauce, fish sauce, and white pepper. Using day-old rice is essential — freshly cooked rice retains too much moisture and clumps during stir-frying, whereas day-old rice separates into individual grains that char slightly in the wok, producing the characteristic smoky 'wok hei' aroma. The seasoning profile distinguishes khao pad from Singaporean fried rice: fish sauce (nam pla) replaces oyster sauce, giving the dish a more savoury-umami, slightly briny background note with a distinct lime finish — a fresh lime wedge and cucumber slices are served alongside as standard accompaniments. The finished dish is lighter in colour and slightly less oily than its Singaporean counterpart. Nutritionally, a 350g plate provides approximately 578 kcal, 29.8g protein, 16.8g fat, and 82.3g carbohydrate. Sodium is clinically significant at ~1,593 mg per plate from the combined fish sauce and soy sauce. The glycemic index is approximately 68 (medium-high), producing a high glycemic load (~56 GL units per plate) that is a concern for patients with type 2 diabetes. Saturated fat is low at 0.9 g/100g. The dish is halal and dairy-free. Key allergens include egg, soy, and fish.
common_user_queries: khao pad vs nasi goreng calories

jasmine rice: ข้าวหอมมะลิ

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from the FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Food Composition Table using the closest available fried rice with chicken and egg entry. No exact khao pad entry was found in a single approved source; values are cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central component data for plausibility. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database range for jasmine rice fried rice. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Thai-style fried rice (khao pad) are not available in FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA FoodData Central at the dish level as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish is not reported in the primary source. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible for a vegetable-oil stir-fried dish with no hydrogenated fat ingredients, consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for analogous fried rice preparations. All core macros (protein_g 8.5, fat_g 4.8, carbohydrate_g 23.5) are non-zero and nutritionally plausible for a jasmine rice dish with chicken and egg. GI of 68 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database range for jasmine rice fried rice (GI 65–72); no direct khao pad GI measurement was found in the database. GL per 350g serving = (68 × 82.25) / 100 ≈ 56.1.
